original_text: (37)	As regards directand indirectbusiness relationships, industry cooperation, industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives can help create additional leverage to identify, mitigate, and prevent adverse impacts. Therefore it should be possible for companies to rely on such initiatives to support the implementation of their due diligence obligations laid down in this Directive to the extent that such schemes and initiatives are appropriate to support the fulfilment of those obligations. Companies could assess, at their own initiative, the alignment of these schemes and initiatives with the obligations under this Directive. In order to ensure full information on such initiatives, the Directive should also refer to the possibility for the Commission and the Member States to facilitate the dissemination of information on such schemes or initiatives and their outcomes. The Commission, in collaboration with Member States, may issue guidance for assessing the fitness of industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives.
amended_text: (37)	As regards direct business relationships, industry cooperation, industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives can help create additional leverage to identify, mitigate, and prevent adverse impacts. Therefore it should be possible for companies to rely on such initiatives to support the implementation of their due diligence obligations laid down in this Directive to the extent that such schemes and initiatives are appropriate to support the fulfilment of those obligations. Companies could assess, at their own initiative, the alignment of these schemes and initiatives with the obligations under this Directive. In order to ensure full information on such initiatives, the Directive should also refer to the possibility for the Commission and the Member States to facilitate the dissemination of information on such schemes or initiatives and their outcomes. The Commission, in collaboration with Member States, may issue guidance for assessing the fitness of industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives.
provision_effect: The amendment removes "and indirect" from the recital, limiting the scope of industry cooperation, schemes, and multi-stakeholder initiatives to support due diligence obligations to direct business relationships only (removing indirect business relationships from this recital).
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Insurance Europe explicitly advocated that "the definition of value chain needs to be reviewed... and limited to established direct business partners," indicating a clear preference for restricting due diligence obligations to direct business relationships. The amendment removes "and indirect" from the recital, narrowing the scope to direct business relationships only, which directly responds to the organisation's stated position.

amended_text: (37)	As regards direct and indirect business relationships, industry cooperation, industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives can help create additional leverage to identify, mitigate, and prevent adverse impacts. Therefore it should be possible for companies to rely on such initiatives to support the implementation of their due diligence obligations laid down in this Directive to the extent that such schemes and initiatives are appropriate to support the fulfilment of those obligations. Companies could assess, at their own initiative, the alignment of these schemes and initiatives with the obligations under this Directive. In order to ensure full information on such initiatives, the Directive should also refer to the possibility for the Commission and the Member States to facilitate the dissemination of information on such schemes or initiatives and their outcomes. The Commission, in collaboration with Member States,shallissue guidance for assessing the fitness of industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives.
provision_effect: The amendment changes the Commission's issuance of guidance for assessing industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives from discretionary ("may") to mandatory ("shall").
label: NOISE
reasoning: Insurance Europe's position welcomes the recognition of industry schemes and multi-stakeholder initiatives in general terms, but does not express any specific directional stance on whether the Commission's guidance on assessing such schemes should be mandatory or discretionary. Their advocacy focuses on other issues (consistency with sectoral legislation, value chain definition, civil liability, thresholds), making this a general endorsement without a directional stance on the "may" vs. "shall" distinction the amendment addresses.

source_text: If civil liability rules are retained in the Directive, due diligence requirements should be refined so that they are appropriate, clear, proportionate, and achievable. Furthermore, should be amended to clarify that: ◼ The primary responsibility to pay damages rests with the party actually causing the damage, and any damages paid based on the failure to meet due diligence obligations is secondary to it. ◼ The burden of demonstrating that a company has not complied with its due diligence obligations rests on the claiming party. The onus should not be on the company itself to demonstrate its innocence. Similar to , the concerns listed above also apply to . The obligation to pay damages to affected groups (.3(a)) does not meet the requirement of adequate predictability and should be removed. The obligation is not based on any objective criteria, in particular the significance and scale of the damage. Due diligence requirements ◼ Measures for preventing, mitigating and bringing adverse impacts to an end: ◼ Impacts cannot be prevented or mitigated before they have been identified. It is, therefore, unreasonable to require prevention or mitigation measures to impacts that should have been identified ((1)). ◼ Clarification is needed on the type of measures deemed “appropriate” from a financial services perspective for an entity to prevent or bring to an end the adverse impacts
context_before: ◼ The United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGPs) that specifically state that a company is not required to remediate for adverse impacts it has not caused or contributed to, even if they are directly linked to its operations. ◼ There is no centralised body to coordinate multiple payment of damages by various companies to the same affected group. As a result, the provisions in Article 22 would expose the companies concerned and their liability insurers to unaccountable and unpredictable legal risks. Given the vague description of the requirements to prevent adverse impacts and to bring actual adverse impacts to an end, many potential claimants would be encouraged to file civil actions against the companies. Irrespective of these unintended consequences, Insurance Europe points out that injured persons could bring forward claims in accordance with the established principles and rules of international civil law. Hence, it is not clear that there is an existing lack of remedy that needs to be addressed by the Directive. Insurance Europe takes the view that the powers granted to the supervisory authorities without civil liability would be sufficient for the effective enforcement of the Directive. If civil liability rules are retained in the Directive, due diligence requirements should be refined so that they are appropriate, clear, proportionate, and achievable
context_after: ◼ Clarification is needed on the type of measures deemed “appropriate” from a financial services perspective for an entity to prevent or bring to an end the adverse impacts. In fact, in the contractual relationship with policyholders, insurers do not act as “instructing partners” and have thus very limited room for action to influence their behaviours. This is also true for 4
original_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability ofcompaniesfor damages arising due to its failure to comply with the due diligence process. Thecompanyshould be liable for damages if they failed to comply with the obligations to prevent and mitigate potential adverse impacts or to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.
amended_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability ofundertakingsfor damages arising due to its failure to comply with the due diligence process. Theundertakingshould be liable for damages if they failed to comply with the obligations to prevent and mitigate potential adverse impacts or to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.The undertaking should also be liable for damages that result from adverse human rights, good governance, environmental or climate impacts that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or their extent minimised by its subsidiaries.
provision_effect: The amendment extends civil liability to cover damages resulting from adverse impacts caused by subsidiaries, adding a new sentence making the undertaking liable for harms that should have been identified, prevented, or mitigated by its subsidiaries.
label: OPPOSING
reasoning: Insurance Europe explicitly opposes broad civil liability provisions, arguing they expose companies and their insurers to "unaccountable and unpredictable legal risks," and advocates for removing or narrowing civil liability obligations. The amendment moves in the opposite direction by expanding liability to cover subsidiary-related adverse impacts, further broadening the scope of civil liability that Insurance Europe sought to limit or eliminate.

original_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability of companies for damages arising due to its failure to comply with the due diligence process. The company should be liable for damages if they failed to comply with the obligations to prevent and mitigatepotentialadverse impacts or to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.
amended_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability of companies for damages arising due to its failure to comply with the due diligence process. The company should be liable for damages if they failed to comply with the obligations to prevent and mitigate adverse impactsthat could be anticipated in line with existing knowledgeor to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.
provision_effect: The amendment narrows the liability trigger for prevention/mitigation obligations by adding the qualifier "that could be anticipated in line with existing knowledge," replacing the broader reference to "potential" adverse impacts.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Insurance Europe explicitly argued that due diligence requirements should be "appropriate, clear, proportionate, and achievable" and specifically criticised the unreasonableness of requiring prevention or mitigation of impacts "that should have been identified," calling for refinement of these vague obligations. The amendment directly responds to this by adding a foreseeability/knowledge-based qualifier ("that could be anticipated in line with existing knowledge"), making the liability standard clearer and more bounded — precisely the direction Insurance Europe advocated for.

amended_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability of companies for damages arising due to its failure to comply with the due diligence process. The company should be liable for damages if they failed to comply with the obligations to prevent and mitigate potential adverse impacts or to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.The burden of proving compliance with obligations to prevent and mitigate potential adverse impacts or bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent should lie with the respondent company.
provision_effect: The amendment adds a sentence to recital 56 placing the burden of proof of compliance on the respondent company, rather than on the claimant.
label: OPPOSING
reasoning: Insurance Europe explicitly stated that "the burden of demonstrating that a company has not complied with its due diligence obligations rests on the claiming party" and that "the onus should not be on the company itself to demonstrate its innocence." The amendment does the exact opposite, placing the burden of proving compliance squarely on the respondent company, directly contradicting the organisation's expressed position.

amended_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability of companies for damages arising due to itsintentional or gross negligentfailure to comply with the due diligence process. The company should be liable for damagesthat they directly causedif theyintentionally or gross negligentlyfailed to comply with the obligations to prevent and mitigate potential adverse impacts or to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact thatit directly caused and thatshould have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.
justification: Liability should be limited to civil actions based on actual harms that have a direct causal link to the company in question, meaning whether they directly caused the damage. Companies cannot be expected to take full responsibility for actions they did not take.
provision_effect: The amendment narrows civil liability to cases of intentional or gross negligent failure and limits damages to those directly caused by the company, adding a direct causal link requirement that was absent in the original.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Insurance Europe explicitly argued that primary responsibility should rest with the party actually causing the damage, that civil liability provisions were too vague and exposed companies to unpredictable risks, and that the UNGPs do not require remediation for impacts a company did not cause or contribute to. The amendment directly responds to these concerns by restricting liability to intentional or gross negligent conduct and requiring a direct causal link between the company's actions and the damage, which is precisely the narrowing and clarification Insurance Europe advocated for.

source_text: If civil liability rules are retained in the Directive, due diligence requirements should be refined so that they are appropriate, clear, proportionate, and achievable. Furthermore, should be amended to clarify that: ◼ The primary responsibility to pay damages rests with the party actually causing the damage, and any damages paid based on the failure to meet due diligence obligations is secondary to it. ◼ The burden of demonstrating that a company has not complied with its due diligence obligations rests on the claiming party. The onus should not be on the company itself to demonstrate its innocence. Similar to , the concerns listed above also apply to . The obligation to pay damages to affected groups (.3(a)) does not meet the requirement of adequate predictability and should be removed. The obligation is not based on any objective criteria, in particular the significance and scale of the damage. Due diligence requirements ◼ Measures for preventing, mitigating and bringing adverse impacts to an end: ◼ Impacts cannot be prevented or mitigated before they have been identified. It is, therefore, unreasonable to require prevention or mitigation measures to impacts that should have been identified ((1)). ◼ Clarification is needed on the type of measures deemed “appropriate” from a financial services perspective for an entity to prevent or bring to an end the adverse impacts
context_before: ◼ The United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGPs) that specifically state that a company is not required to remediate for adverse impacts it has not caused or contributed to, even if they are directly linked to its operations. ◼ There is no centralised body to coordinate multiple payment of damages by various companies to the same affected group. As a result, the provisions in Article 22 would expose the companies concerned and their liability insurers to unaccountable and unpredictable legal risks. Given the vague description of the requirements to prevent adverse impacts and to bring actual adverse impacts to an end, many potential claimants would be encouraged to file civil actions against the companies. Irrespective of these unintended consequences, Insurance Europe points out that injured persons could bring forward claims in accordance with the established principles and rules of international civil law. Hence, it is not clear that there is an existing lack of remedy that needs to be addressed by the Directive. Insurance Europe takes the view that the powers granted to the supervisory authorities without civil liability would be sufficient for the effective enforcement of the Directive. If civil liability rules are retained in the Directive, due diligence requirements should be refined so that they are appropriate, clear, proportionate, and achievable
context_after: ◼ Clarification is needed on the type of measures deemed “appropriate” from a financial services perspective for an entity to prevent or bring to an end the adverse impacts. In fact, in the contractual relationship with policyholders, insurers do not act as “instructing partners” and have thus very limited room for action to influence their behaviours. original_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability of companies for damages arising due to its failure to comply with the due diligence process. The company should be liable for damages if they failed to comply with the obligations to prevent and mitigate potential adverse impacts or to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.
amended_text: (56)	In order to ensure effective compensation of victims of adverse impacts, Member States should be required to lay down rules governing the civil liability of companies for damages arising due to its failure to comply with the due diligence process. The company should be liable for damages if they failed to comply with the obligations to prevent, prioritiseand mitigate potential adverse impacts or to bring actual impacts to an end and minimise their extent, and as a result of this failure an adverse impact that should have been identified,prioritised,prevented, mitigated, brought to an end or its extent minimised through the appropriate measures occurred and led to damage.Nonetheless, in cases where, as a consequence of the duty of prioritisation, the severe adverse impact arises from a lower prioritised adverse impact that was not yet addressed, the company should not be held liable, always provided that the prioritisation was adequate according to the severity, urgency and the likelihood of the adverse impacts.
provision_effect: The amendment adds a "prioritisation" obligation to the due diligence process and introduces a liability exemption for companies when a severe adverse impact arises from a lower-prioritised impact not yet addressed, provided the prioritisation was adequate.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Insurance Europe explicitly called for civil liability rules to be refined so that due diligence requirements are "appropriate, clear, proportionate, and achievable," and specifically flagged that it is "unreasonable to require prevention or mitigation measures to impacts that should have been identified." The amendment's new liability carve-out — shielding companies from liability when a severe impact arises from a lower-prioritised impact not yet addressed — directly responds to this concern by introducing proportionality and predictability into the liability framework, moving in the direction Insurance Europe advocated for.

source_text: Page 4 of 8 Comments on the proposal for a Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive and duration. A business relationship is deemed to be “established” as long as it does not represent a negligible or merely ancillary part of the value chain. We therefore consider only those business relationships to be subject to due diligence obligations whose activities are required for provision of the financial service. By contrast, we would classify activities within a company’s own business sector and those of suppliers that are not related to the provision of the financial service as a “negligible or merely ancillary part of the value chain”. We furthermore request it to be made clear that refinancing, treasury and derivatives business, including business relationships with other market players associated with these activities, is not covered by the due diligence obligations within a company’s own business sector. Avoidance of additional risks through civil liability Banks only have a limited view of the production conditions of their clients or their suppliers. It is not possible to rule out the establishment of civil liability leading to a shift in liability risks in the overall supply chain from the actual causer of a breach of due diligence obligations to the financial institutions
context_before: We fundamentally welcome this, as due diligence following the payment of loans is largely not enforceable. However, Article 7(5) and (6) and Article 8(7) also propose in the event of a breach of certain provisions by the client that the business relationship with the client be terminated (in the absence of significant reasons precluding this), which implies due diligence throughout the entire term of the contract. It should be unequivocally stated here that one-time due diligence prior to lending is sufficient. The question also arises as to the impact of these provisions with regard to questions of liability and potential financial penalties (see Articles 20 and 22 of the proposal). It must also be made clear that a renewed identification of actual or potential adverse impacts with regard to a client is not necessary in the event of a prolongation, novation or comparable actions if an already existing contractual relationship is being continued. Furthermore, an explicit grandfathering clause should be introduced so that only new commitments of financial institutions are subject to the due diligence obligations. Limitation of due diligence obligations The “established business relationship” defined in Article 3(f) of the proposal requires further explanation stating which aspects are to be taken into account for determining the intensity
context_after: Companies should not be held liable for damages in their supply chain if they have not caused them directly, could not reasonably know of them or if they have taken appropriate due diligence measures to prevent them. In order to avoid additional liability risks for companies, the German legislator has opted against including proprietary civil liability in the national Act on Corporate Due Diligence Obligations in Supply Chains. However, there are extensive sanctions available at the national level. We accordingly also do not consider the inclusion of civil liability in the European Commission proposal and the associated additional liability risks for companies to be necessary and suggest removing the provision concerning civil liability. Responsible corporate governance also without directors’ duties pertaining to sustainability The directors’ duties pertaining to sustainability arising from Articles 15 and 25 of the proposal should not conflict with the directors’ general duties and their alignment with the interests of the company. At the same time there is no doubt in our view that environmental and social factors in particular also belong to the issues to be taken into account for responsible corporate governance. The provisions ought therefore to be worded in a less binding manner. The Regulatory Scrutiny Board of the Commission has for the same reasons repeatedly criticised its legislative efforts with regard to sustainable corporate governance
original_text: (20)	In order to allow companies to properly identify the adverse impacts in their value chain and to make it possible for them to exercise appropriate leverage, the due diligence obligations should be limited in this Directive to established business relationships. For the purpose of this Directive, established business relationships should mean such directand indirectbusiness relationships which are, or which are expected to be lasting,in view of their intensity and duration and which do not represent a negligible or ancillary part of thevaluechain. The nature of business relationships as “established” should be reassessed periodically, and at least every 12 months.If the direct business relationship of a company is established, then all linked indirect business relationships should also be considered as established regarding that company.
amended_text: (20)	In order to allow companies to properly identify the adverse impacts in their value chain and to make it possible for them to exercise appropriate leverage, the due diligence obligations should be limited in this Directive to established business relationships. For the purpose of this Directive, established business relationships should mean such direct business relationships which are in view of their intensity and duration and which do not represent a negligible or ancillary part of thesupplychain. The nature of business relationships as “established” should be reassessed periodically, and at least every 12 months.
provision_effect: The amendment removes indirect business relationships from the scope of "established business relationships" and replaces "value chain" with "supply chain," narrowing the due diligence obligations to direct business relationships only.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The GBIC explicitly advocates for limiting due diligence obligations to business relationships directly tied to the provision of financial services, classifying unrelated activities as "negligible or ancillary," and argues that indirect business relationships (e.g., refinancing, treasury, derivatives) should not be covered. The amendment removes indirect business relationships from the definition of "established business relationships," directly narrowing the scope in the direction GBIC advocated for.

source_text: It is also necessary to ensure that smaller and medium-sized enterprises in particular are granted sufficient time to implement their obligations. Guidance for companies and financial institutions We welcome the endeavours of the European Commission to provide the companies affected with comprehensive guidance for implementation of the due diligence obligations arising from the directive. For example, of the proposal provides for the development and provision of model contractual clauses by the European Commission. We fundamentally welcome this initiative. However, it is necessary here to clarify to what extent it will be ensured that the model contractual clauses of the European Commission are recognised under national law as sufficient for compliance with national implementation laws. It is important for these model contractual clauses to be made available as part of the directive rather than only being developed at a later stage, as all changes to financial contracts can only be implemented at great financial cost. Furthermore, companies should be deemed CSDDD-compliant if they make use of and/or adjust these model contractual clauses in their existing business relationships.
context_before: It is furthermore necessary to make clear here that ordinary notice of termination is sufficient for ending the business relationship and termination with immediate effect is not required. The latter could otherwise give rise to substantial conflicts with the requirements for smooth business continuation at banks. We consider the rights, prohibitions and obligations set out in the annex to the directive to be generally too broad. It should be borne in mind that these are by their nature addressed to states and not companies organised under private law. Environmental due diligence obligations in accordance with existing provisions Climate protection and environmental aspects already play a vital role today in the business model of financial institutions. For example, larger financial institutions in particular have committed themselves via the voluntary commitment of the German financial sector, their own voluntary commitments or their membership of the Net Zero Banking Alliance (NZBA) to act in accordance with the Paris climate goals and set themselves their own net zero targets. The requirements arising from Article 15 concerning transition plans for companies to comply with the Paris climate protection goals must be in line with existing and future obligations from the NZBA and CSRD. It is also necessary to ensure that smaller and medium-sized enterprises in particular are granted sufficient time to implement their obligations
context_after: Page 6 of 8 Comments on the proposal for a Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive Moreover, the European Commission is to provide guidelines to support companies and member states in fulfilling their due diligence obligations (Article 13 of the proposal). We consider it urgently necessary for the specific features of the banking industry also to be taken into account in the development of such guidelines, especially the existing banking supervisory requirements with regard to ESG risks and the fact that direct information and due diligence obligations can only be agreed for banks in the direct contractual relationship with the borrower, so that the due diligence obligations can only apply to the direct borrower. In addition, the guidelines should be issued as soon as possible and before the directive enters into force. The general exemption of SMEs from the directive’s scope of application is a step in the right direction. However, all SMEs will be strongly affected by the “trickle-down” effect as many of them are in the supply chain of large enterprises. SMEs must receive effective support regardless of whether or not they fall within the scope of application of a binding framework. Whether the envisaged supporting measures really can provide relief here is something that must be questioned. Substantiation of these measures is desirable
original_text: (45)	In order to facilitate companies’ compliance with their due diligence requirements through their value chain and limiting shifting compliance burden on SME business partners, the Commission should provide guidance on model contractual clauses.
amended_text: (45)	In order to facilitate companies’ compliance with their due diligence requirements through their value chain and limiting shifting compliance burden on SME business partners, the Commission should provide, within the twelve months following the entry into force of the Directive,guidance on model contractual clauses.
provision_effect: The amendment adds a specific deadline of twelve months following the entry into force of the Directive for the Commission to provide guidance on model contractual clauses, where the original set no timeline.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The GBIC explicitly stated that model contractual clauses should be made available "as part of the directive rather than only being developed at a later stage" and that guidelines "should be issued as soon as possible and before the directive enters into force," directly advocating for an earlier, time-bound delivery of the guidance. The amendment responds to this by imposing a concrete twelve-month deadline, moving the text in the direction the organisation advocated for.

original_text: (45)	In order to facilitate companies’ compliance with their due diligence requirementsthroughtheirvaluechain and limiting shifting compliance burden onSMEbusiness partners, the Commission should provide guidance on model contractual clauses.
amended_text: (45)	In order to facilitate companies’ compliance with their due diligence requirementsas regardstheirsupplychain and limiting shifting compliance burden on business partners, in particular SMEs, the Commission should provide guidance on model contractual clauses.
provision_effect: The amendment narrows the scope from "value chain" to "supply chain" and reframes the SME protection as applying to "business partners, in particular SMEs" rather than "SME business partners."
label: NOISE
reasoning: The GBIC's matched text welcomes the Commission's provision of model contractual clauses and raises concerns about their legal recognition and timing, but does not take any directional stance on whether the scope should be "value chain" vs. "supply chain" or on how SMEs should be positioned in the recital's wording. Their general welcome of the model contractual clauses initiative is not specific enough to align with or oppose the particular textual changes the amendment makes.

source_text: Clarification is needed here as to how the “appropriate resources” are to be defined. - (2)(d) and (3)(e) envisage targeted and proportionate support measures for SMEs with which an established business relationship exists. It is unclear whether support measures are also to be provided for corresponding clients of financial services companies. - (3) and (4) of the proposal refer to the option of concluding a contract with an indirect business partner with a view to achieving compliance with a code of conduct or prevention action plan. It is not clear to what extent the conclusion of a contract with indirect service providers is feasible and sensible. - There is also a need to clarify the relationship between the damages and other financial compensation paid by companies to affected persons or communities pursuant to
context_before: - In addition, the mandatory due diligence obligations for regulated financial services should be limited to clients that themselves are not regulated financial service providers. Owing to the broad spectrum, large number and complexity of business relationships within the financial sector, application of the directive to business relations in the interbank market and in the area of securities clearing and settlement, for example, would entail a disproportionate amount of time and effort. The added value would also be limited as regulated financial undertakings are by definition already subject to harmonised regulation and supervision, also with regard to ESG risks. - Article 3(h) defines the expression “independent third-party verification”. Independent third-party verification can – as can recourse to suitable industry initiatives – be an appropriate measure for verifying compliance with contractual assurances for preventing or bringing to an end adverse impacts. We welcome the fact that the proposal does not envisage any further external third-party audit requirements for compliance with the due diligence obligations. - Companies are to be entitled to make use of appropriate resources for investigating actual and potential adverse impacts, including independent reports and information gathered through the complaints procedure (Article 6(4)). Clarification is needed here as to how the “appropriate resources” are to be defined
context_after: Page 8 of 8 Comments on the proposal for a Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive Article 8(3) of the proposal and potential liability for damages pursuant to Article 22. The question arises here as to the extent to which payments resulting from Article 8 of the proposal are taken into account as mitigating factors when determining the scope of a potential liability for damages. - Concerning the accompanying measures envisaged in Article 14, the statements about which precise information and support measures are to be provided on the websites, platforms and portals operated by the member states pursuant to Article 14(1) of the proposal remain vague. Here we would like to see further substantiation of the information to be published. - We see an attempt to enshrine in law the provisions of the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises by reference to the annex to the draft directive. However, it should be borne in mind that these are not binding and therefore are intended neither to be enforced by the authorities, nor to serve as the foundation for judicial damages. It appears that not all these limitations inherent in the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises have been sufficiently taken into account so far in the legislative procedure for the Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive.
original_text: (e)	providetargeted and proportionatesupport foran SME with which the company has an establishedbusinessrelationship, where compliance with the code of conduct or the corrective action plan would jeopardise the viability of theSME;
amended_text: (e)	providethe necessarysupportsuch as loans or financingfor businesspartners, in particular for SMEs, where compliance with the code of conduct or the corrective action plan would jeopardise the viability of thebusiness partner;
provision_effect: The amendment replaces "targeted and proportionate support for an SME with an established business relationship" with "necessary support such as loans or financing for business partners, in particular for SMEs," broadening the scope from SMEs with established relationships to all business partners and specifying the type of support (loans or financing).
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The GBIC explicitly flags uncertainty about whether support measures under this provision apply to clients of financial services companies, and questions the scope of "established business relationship" — suggesting they want clarification on the provision's reach. The amendment broadens the scope to all business partners (not just SMEs with established relationships) and specifies financial instruments like loans, which partially addresses the GBIC's concern about applicability to financial sector clients, but the GBIC's position does not clearly advocate for either broadening or narrowing the provision — only for clarification — making the directional alignment genuinely ambiguous.

source_text: So we fear the negative effects on SMEs since enterprises in the scope of the Directive will have to identify potential adverse effects in their supply chain. SMEunited appreciates the fact that large enterprises will be required to provide support to SMEs in their supply chain. However, we have concerns whether and how it will be the case. Equally, the obligation to produce a yearly assessment report detailing the implementation of the due diligence obligations will have a serious impact on SMEs and imposes additional administrative burdens in addition to the reporting obligations of the CSRD. The proposal also introduces a “duty of care” for directors of companies, which obliges to adopt an action plan to explain their business model and show that their business strategy is compatible with a sustainable economy. This action plan should then be taken into account in the remuneration of company managers. Again, the exclusion of SMEs of the scope is more than justified, as this is not adapted to the reality of our small companies, mainly family businesses. SMEunited also welcomes that Member States and the Commission will have to provide accompanying measures and tools for SMEs. This is a longstanding request from SMEunited, and we are grateful that the Commission has recognised the need. The accompanying measures proposed, such as a dedicated website, platforms, or portals, are necessary, but one should make sure that they can really give practical guidance to SMEs
context_before: The consultation process with our member organisations is still going on. We will finalise our position paper in the coming days. However we can already provide the following preliminary remarks. In general the Due Diligence Directive leaves too much freedom to the Member States to legislate further, so it won’t be possible to create a level playing field and a harmonised due diligence framework within the Internal Market. The directive does also not provide legal certainty. The material norms are unclear and unfit for application by SMEs. The European Commission rightly opted to exclude SMEs from the scope of the Directive. As SMEunited stressed already in the past, the financial and administrative burden to set up and implement a due diligence process would be extremely high and impact SMEs disproportionally. In addition, they do not have the necessary know-how and market power.  SMEunited appreciates that the Commission is fully aware and recognises that the financial and administrative burden to set up and implement a due diligence process would be extremely high for SMEs. This is also mentioned in the UN Guiding Principles and the OECD Guidance. However, the provisions in the directive that should shield SMEs from disproportionate requirements don’t provide any certainty. So we fear the negative effects on SMEs since enterprises in the scope of the Directive will have to identify potential adverse effects in their supply chain
context_after: The accompanying measures proposed, such as a dedicated website, platforms, or portals, are necessary, but one should make sure that they can really give practical guidance to SMEs. Academic research in this field proves that none of the existing tools is accessible for SMEs: They are overly complex, sometimes contain hundreds of pages and are written in a complex language. It must be guaranteed that these tools are available no later than when the directive will enter into force. These measures have to be developed in close cooperation with the SME organisations. SMEunited is offering all its support and involvement. The proposed model clauses also leave a lot of room for interpretation. There are fears that large enterprises will shift their due diligence obligations to their SMEs providers.
original_text: (47)	Although SMEs are not included in the scope of this Directive, they could be impacted by its provisions as contractors or subcontractors to the companies which are in the scope. The aim is nevertheless to mitigate financial or administrative burden on SMEs, many of which are already struggling in the context of the global economic and sanitary crisis. In order to support SMEs, Member States should set up and operate, either individually or jointly, dedicated websites, portals or platforms, and Member States could also financially support SMEs and help them build capacity. Such support should also be made accessible, and where necessary adapted and extended to upstream economic operators in third countries. Companies whose business partner is an SME, are also encouraged to support them to comply with due diligence measures, in case such requirements would jeopardize the viability of the SME and use fair, reasonable, non-discriminatory and proportionate requirements vis-a-vis the SMEs.
amended_text: (47)	Although SMEs are not included in the scope of this Directive, they could be impacted by its provisions as contractors or subcontractors to the companies which are in the scope. The aim is nevertheless to mitigate financial or administrative burden on SMEs, many of which are already struggling in the context of the global economic and sanitary crisis. In order to support SMEs, Member States should set up and operate, either individually or jointly, dedicated websites, portals or platforms, and Member States could also financially support SMEs and help them build capacity. Such support should also be made accessible, and where necessary adapted and extended to upstream economic operators in third countries. Companies whose business partner is an SME, are also encouraged to support them to comply with due diligence measures, in case such requirements would jeopardize the viability of the SME and use fair, reasonable, non-discriminatory and proportionate requirements vis-a-vis the SMEs.Some SMEs might want to voluntarily apply the due diligence framework. They should be incentivized to do so and rewarded for doing so. Member states should, for instance, adapt their rating systems for public procurements so that candidates and tenderers who are not in the scope of the directive, notably SMEs, but do comply with due diligence obligation schemes are rated more positively than other tenderers who do not. Member states, professional and sectoral organisations are encouraged to set up labelling systems to identify which companies falling out of the scope of this Directive apply certified due diligence schemes equivalent to the obligations laid out in this Directive.
provision_effect: The amendment adds new text to recital 47 encouraging SMEs to voluntarily apply due diligence frameworks, incentivizing them through public procurement rating advantages and labelling systems for SMEs that comply with certified due diligence schemes equivalent to the Directive's obligations.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: SMEunited strongly supports excluding SMEs from the scope of the Directive due to disproportionate burdens, and welcomes accompanying measures for SMEs. However, the amendment introduces voluntary participation incentives (procurement ratings, labelling) for SMEs — a direction that neither clearly aligns with nor opposes SMEunited's stated position. SMEunited does not advocate for or against voluntary SME participation schemes, and while they welcome support measures, it is genuinely unclear whether they would endorse incentivizing SMEs to voluntarily take on due diligence obligations they otherwise fought to be excluded from.

It must be guaranteed that these tools are available no later than when the directive will enter into force. These measures have to be developed in close cooperation with the SME organisations. SMEunited is offering all its support and involvement. The proposed model clauses also leave a lot of room for interpretation. There are fears that large enterprises will shift their due diligence obligations to their SMEs providers.
original_text: (47)	Although SMEs are not included in the scope of this Directive, they could be impacted by its provisions as contractors or subcontractors to the companies which are in the scope. The aim is nevertheless to mitigate financial or administrative burden on SMEs, many of which are already struggling in the context of the global economic and sanitary crisis. In order to support SMEs, Member States should set up and operate, either individually or jointly, dedicated websites, portals or platforms, and Member Statescouldalso financially support SMEs and help them build capacity. Such support shouldalsobe made accessible, and where necessary adapted and extended to upstream economic operators in third countries. Companies whose business partner is an SME,are also encouraged tosupport them to comply with due diligence measures, in case such requirements would jeopardize the viability of the SME and use fair, reasonable, non-discriminatory and proportionate requirements vis-a-vis the SMEs.
amended_text: (47)	Although SMEs are not included in the scope of this Directive, they could beheavilyimpacted by its provisions as contractors or subcontractors to the companies which are in the scope. The aim is nevertheless to mitigatethe immensefinancial or administrative burden on SMEs, many of which are already struggling in the context of the global economic and sanitary crisis. In order to supportcompanies in the implementation, includingSMEs, Member States should set up and operate, either individually or jointly, dedicated websites, portals or platforms,to provide information and support to companies,and Member Statesshouldalso financially support SMEsspecificallyand help them build capacity. Such support should be made accessible, and where necessary adapted and extended to upstream economic operators in third countries. Companies whose business partner is an SME,should alsosupport them to comply with due diligence measures, in case such requirements would jeopardize the viability of the SME and use fair, reasonable, non-discriminatory and proportionate requirements vis-a-vis the SMEs.

label: ALIGNED
reasoning: SMEunited explicitly advocates for stronger protections for SMEs, expresses concern that the provisions shielding SMEs "don't provide any certainty," and welcomes accompanying measures while stressing they must be practically accessible and guaranteed. The amendment directly responds to these concerns by strengthening the language on the burden SMEs face, making Member State financial support mandatory rather than optional, and converting the encouragement for large companies to support SMEs into an obligation — all directions SMEunited specifically advocated for.

source_text: So we fear the negative effects on SMEs since enterprises in the scope of the Directive will have to identify potential adverse effects in their supply chain. SMEunited appreciates the fact that large enterprises will be required to provide support to SMEs in their supply chain. However, we have concerns whether and how it will be the case. Equally, the obligation to produce a yearly assessment report detailing the implementation of the due diligence obligations will have a serious impact on SMEs and imposes additional administrative burdens in addition to the reporting obligations of the CSRD. The proposal also introduces a “duty of care” for directors of companies, which obliges to adopt an action plan to explain their business model and show that their business strategy is compatible with a sustainable economy. This action plan should then be taken into account in the remuneration of company managers. Again, the exclusion of SMEs of the scope is more than justified, as this is not adapted to the reality of our small companies, mainly family businesses. SMEunited also welcomes that Member States and the Commission will have to provide accompanying measures and tools for SMEs. This is a longstanding request from SMEunited, and we are grateful that the Commission has recognised the need. The accompanying measures proposed, such as a dedicated website, platforms, or portals, are necessary, but one should make sure that they can really give practical guidance to SMEs
context_before: The consultation process with our member organisations is still going on. We will finalise our position paper in the coming days. However we can already provide the following preliminary remarks. In general the Due Diligence Directive leaves too much freedom to the Member States to legislate further, so it won’t be possible to create a level playing field and a harmonised due diligence framework within the Internal Market. The directive does also not provide legal certainty. The material norms are unclear and unfit for application by SMEs. The European Commission rightly opted to exclude SMEs from the scope of the Directive. As SMEunited stressed already in the past, the financial and administrative burden to set up and implement a due diligence process would be extremely high and impact SMEs disproportionally. In addition, they do not have the necessary know-how and market power.  SMEunited appreciates that the Commission is fully aware and recognises that the financial and administrative burden to set up and implement a due diligence process would be extremely high for SMEs. This is also mentioned in the UN Guiding Principles and the OECD Guidance. However, the provisions in the directive that should shield SMEs from disproportionate requirements don’t provide any certainty. So we fear the negative effects on SMEs since enterprises in the scope of the Directive will have to identify potential adverse effects in their supply chain

original_text: (34)	So as to comply with the prevention and mitigation obligation under this Directive, companies should be required to take the following actions, where relevant. Where necessary due to the complexity of prevention measures, companies should develop and implement a prevention action plan. Companiesshouldseek to obtain contractual assurances from a direct partner with whom they havean establishedbusiness relationship that it will ensure compliance with the code of conduct or the prevention action plan, including by seeking correspondingcontractualassurances from its partners to the extent that their activities are part of the companies’valuechain. Thecontractualassurancesshould beaccompanied by appropriate measures to verify compliance. To ensure comprehensive prevention of actual and potential adverse impacts, companies should also make investments which aim to prevent adverse impacts, provide targeted and proportionate support for an SME with which they havean establishedbusiness relationship such as financing, for example, through direct financing, low-interest loans, guarantees of continued sourcing, and assistance in securing financing, to help implement the code of conduct or prevention action plan, or technical guidance such as in the form of training, management systems upgrading, and collaborate with other companies.
amended_text: (34)	So as to comply with the prevention and mitigation obligation under this Directive, companies should be required to take the following actions, where relevantdepending on the circumstances and their leverage. Where necessary due to the complexity of prevention measures, companies should develop and implement a prevention action plan. Companiesmayseek to obtain contractualor otherassurances from a direct partner with whom they havea directbusiness relationshipoutside the EUthat it will ensure compliance with the code of conduct or the prevention action plan, including by seeking corresponding assurances from its partners to the extent that their activities are part of the companies’supplychainwhere possible. The assurancesmay be, where appropriate,accompanied by appropriate measures to verify compliance. To ensure comprehensive prevention of actual and potential adverse impacts, companies should also make investments which aim to prevent adverse impacts,and, where appropriate, collaborate with other companies to that extent. Companies should alsoprovide targeted and proportionate support for an SME with which they havea directbusiness relationshipoutside the EUsuch as financing, for example, through direct financing, low-interest loans, guarantees of continued sourcing, and assistance in securing financing, to help implement the code of conduct or prevention action plan, or technical guidance such as in the form of training, management systems upgrading, and collaborate with other companies.
justification: Contractual assurance can be one tool of due diligence but should not be the only one.
provision_effect: The amendment weakens the obligation to seek contractual assurances from direct partners by changing "should seek" to "may seek," limiting it to direct business relationships outside the EU, and making verification measures optional rather than expected.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: SMEunited explicitly fears that large enterprises will shift their due diligence obligations onto SME suppliers through contractual mechanisms, and expresses concern that the provisions shielding SMEs from disproportionate requirements lack certainty. The amendment directly responds to this by softening the contractual assurance obligation (from "should" to "may"), narrowing its geographic scope to outside the EU, and making verification optional — all of which reduce the risk of compliance burdens being cascaded down to SMEs in the supply chain.

source_text: Concepts imprecise The key definitions of the proposed Directive, such as “business relationship” and “established business relationship”, “value chain”, “stakeholder” and “appropriate measure”, are not defined with sufficient precision. In addition, the value chain, for example, has been defined to be so broad that companies' ability to control its entire value chain is at least challenging and virtually impossible to implement. In particular, the concept of “established business relationship” is a completely new terminology that is not found in the UN Guiding Principles or the OECD Guidelines, which are familiar to companies and on which they have been working for years. The concept needs to be defined more clearly and should be limited to direct contract suppliers, not the whole value chain. In order to realistically achieve the objectives of the Directive, the due diligence requirement should distinguish between more comprehensive requirements for direct suppliers and more reasonable requirements for indirect suppliers. Mandatory (comprehensive) requirements should be limited to those parts of the supply chain with which the company has a direct contractual relationship
context_before: Finnish cooperatives have already done a lot to integrate responsible practices into their business, and sustainability is an increasingly important and strong part of their strategies. Human rights and environmental issues are important for cooperative businesses and a harmonized EU legislation that genuinely helps companies to improve the way they operate in their value chains is very welcome. However, there is still a lot of work to be done to clarify the content of the regulation, and we would like to express our concern about the proposal being too open to interpretation. There are many difficult issues regarding the scope and liability issues at the practical level. The Directive, which is currently subject to a lot of interpretation, combined with the fact that the proposal leaves many practical issues to be decided at Member State level, also means that, although the aim is to have a harmonized European corporate sustainability legislation, the content of corporate due diligence will vary from one EU country to another, and the objective of coherency does not appear to be sufficiently realized. In order to achieve the key objectives of the proposed Directive, regulation should be predictable, and the obligations imposed by it sufficiently precise, in particular because the proposal for a Directive also includes proposals for sanctions for infringements and for liability for damages
context_after: As this will be binding legislation, the Directive must be so clear and precise that companies will have both the de facto ability to identify the measures required of them and the de facto ability to act according to the legal requirements and to ensure that their activities comply with the due diligence requirement. Impact of the proposal on SMEs The impact of the proposal on SMEs should also be assessed. Although they are not subject to the due diligence obligation under the proposed Directive, in practice companies that fall under the scope will pass on the requirements imposed on them to the SMEs in their value chain. The Directive should therefore specify what the maximum requirements could be, taking into account e.g. SMEs opportunities to act on those requirements. On the other hand, the obligation for large companies to participate in supporting SMEs, as set out in the Directive, should also be defined more precisely, and delimit when the aid should be given. This is why it is very important that the Commission explicitly requires Member States to offer companies information and other support measures so that they can meet the Directive’s requirements. Climate change mitigation obligations are challenging Finnish cooperatives fully support the Paris Agreement and the climate neutrality goal, and most already have ambitious strategies to reduce their CO2 emissions
original_text: (20)	In order to allow companies to properly identify the adverse impacts in theirvaluechain and to make it possible for them to exercise appropriate leverage, the due diligence obligations should be limited in this Directive toestablishedbusiness relationships. For the purpose of this Directive,establishedbusiness relationships should mean such direct and indirect business relationships which are, or which are expected to be lasting, in view of their intensity and duration and which do not represent a negligible or ancillary part of thevaluechain. The nature of business relationships as “established” should be reassessed periodically,andat least every 12 months.If the direct business relationship of a company is established, then all linked indirect business relationships should also be considered as established regarding that company.
amended_text: (20)	In order to allow companies to properly identify the adverse impacts in theirsupplychain and to make it possible for them to exercise appropriate leverage, the due diligence obligations should be limited in this Directive todirect contractualbusiness relationships. For the purpose of this Directive,directbusiness relationships should mean such direct and indirect business relationships which are, or which are expected to be lasting, in view of their intensity and duration and which do not represent a negligible or ancillary part of thesupplychain andwhich link both parties throughalegal contract.
provision_effect: The amendment narrows the scope of due diligence obligations from "established business relationships" across the entire "value chain" to "direct contractual business relationships" within the "supply chain," removing the concept of "established" business relationships and the periodic reassessment requirement, and limiting obligations to relationships backed by a legal contract.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocated that "established business relationship" should be "limited to direct contract suppliers, not the whole value chain" and that "mandatory (comprehensive) requirements should be limited to those parts of the supply chain with which the company has a direct contractual relationship." The amendment does precisely this — replacing "established business relationships" across the "value chain" with "direct contractual business relationships" within the "supply chain," directly responding to the organisation's specific directional demand.

original_text: (20)	In order to allow companies to properly identify the adverse impacts in theirvaluechain and to make it possible for them to exercise appropriate leverage, the due diligence obligations should be limited in this Directive toestablishedbusiness relationships. For the purpose of this Directive,establishedbusiness relationships should mean such directand indirect business relationships which are, or which are expected to be lasting, in view of their intensity and duration and which donot represent a negligible or ancillary part of thevaluechain.The nature of business relationships as “established” should be reassessed periodically, and at least every 12 months. If the direct business relationship of a company is established, then all linked indirect business relationships should also be considered as established regarding that company.

source_text: As this will be binding legislation, the Directive must be so clear and precise that companies will have both the de facto ability to identify the measures required of them and the de facto ability to act according to the legal requirements and to ensure that their activities comply with the due diligence requirement. Impact of the proposal on SMEs The impact of the proposal on SMEs should also be assessed. Although they are not subject to the due diligence obligation under the proposed Directive, in practice companies that fall under the scope will pass on the requirements imposed on them to the SMEs in their value chain. The Directive should therefore specify what the maximum requirements could be, taking into account e.g. SMEs opportunities to act on those requirements. On the other hand, the obligation for large companies to participate in supporting SMEs, as set out in the Directive, should also be defined more precisely, and delimit when the aid should be given. This is why it is very important that the Commission explicitly requires Member States to offer companies information and other support measures so that they can meet the Directive’s requirements. Climate change mitigation obligations are challenging Finnish cooperatives fully support the Paris Agreement and the climate neutrality goal, and most already have ambitious strategies to reduce their CO2 emissions
context_before: Concepts imprecise The key definitions of the proposed Directive, such as “business relationship” and “established business relationship”, “value chain”, “stakeholder” and “appropriate measure”, are not defined with sufficient precision. In addition, the value chain, for example, has been defined to be so broad that companies' ability to control its entire value chain is at least challenging and virtually impossible to implement. In particular, the concept of “established business relationship” is a completely new terminology that is not found in the UN Guiding Principles or the OECD Guidelines, which are familiar to companies and on which they have been working for years. The concept needs to be defined more clearly and should be limited to direct contract suppliers, not the whole value chain. In order to realistically achieve the objectives of the Directive, the due diligence requirement should distinguish between more comprehensive requirements for direct suppliers and more reasonable requirements for indirect suppliers. Mandatory (comprehensive) requirements should be limited to those parts of the supply chain with which the company has a direct contractual relationship
context_after: However, we find Article 15 of the Directive challenging, as the EU already has and is developing climate change and sustainability policies such as the Fit-for-55 package, the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy, which guide and will guide companies. As such, we hope that if the Directive imposes such direct and binding obligations on companies to limit global warming to 1.5 degrees or to link the director's variable remunerations to achieving the goals of the company's climate change strategies, there will be no unnecessary overlaps in the different legislations and the resulting inconsistency shall be avoided at all costs. Implementation of the due diligence obligation at the Group level The Directive proposal does not provide sufficient clarity on how the due diligence obligation is implemented at group level. The current text, and in particular the definition of "company" (Article 3a), seems to indicate that the requirements of the Directive are set at company level and not at group level. This would mean that a company with subsidiaries in other Member States would have to comply with the decisions of several different supervisory authorities. Groups should be able to organize the work required by the due diligence in accordance with their group's business model
original_text: (47)	Although SMEs are not included in the scope of this Directive, they could be impacted by its provisions as contractors or subcontractors to the companies which are in the scope. The aim is nevertheless to mitigate financial or administrative burden on SMEs, many of which are already struggling in the context of the global economic and sanitary crisis. In order to support SMEs, Member States should set up and operate, either individually or jointly, dedicated websites, portals or platforms, and Member States could also financially support SMEs and help them build capacity. Such support should also be made accessible, and where necessary adapted and extended to upstream economic operators in third countries. Companies whose business partner is an SME, are also encouraged to support them to comply with due diligence measures, in case such requirements would jeopardize the viability of the SME and use fair, reasonable, non-discriminatory and proportionate requirements vis-a-vis the SMEs.
